items.title: Apple Accidentally Built a Programming Language and Forgot the Compiler
items.source: Cherri HN discussion (47549824)
items.intent: Provocative platform critique arguing Apple accidentally built a programming language (Turing-complete, exclusive system access) and shipped it without a compiler, text editor, or any real development tools. Uses HN quotes as evidence of community-wide frustration. More opinionated than the technical deep-dive, more systemic than the personal story.

items.title: The Endowment Effect of Privacy
items.source: IDEAS board: What Would You Pay to Un-Share Your Data?
items.intent: Reframe the privacy debate through behavioral economics. The gap between "I have nothing to hide" and "wait, they know WHAT?" is the endowment effect — a documented cognitive bias that companies exploit through onboarding design. Offers a practical takeaway: emotional pre-pricing before granting permissions.

items.title: What Your Expense App Knows — The Permission Slip
items.source: IDEAS board + user brief
items.intent: Trace the chain reaction from a single "Allow SMS access" tap through five layers: raw access, transaction extraction, pattern inference, behavioral profiling, and breach risk. Educational and technical — a builder explaining how the machinery works, not just warning that it exists.

items.source: IDEAS board + user brief
items.intent: Frame the post as a leaked intelligence dossier — the literal profile a buyer would get if they purchased your expense app's database after a breach. Cold, factual, formatted like a report. Then connect to documented breaches where databases this detailed were actually sold. The format is the argument.

items.title: What Your Expense App Knows — The 30-Day Audit
items.source: IDEAS board + user brief

The post makes the data trail tangible and personal — then connects it to real Indian fintech breaches where this exact data ended up for sale.

items.title: Your Balance Is a Lie
items.source: IDEAS board: Safe to Spend reframe
items.intent: Provocative, opinionated take that directly challenges the bank balance as a useful number. Argues it's "everyone else's money that hasn't left yet" and that banks show the big number because the honest number hurts their business. Most confrontational of the three Safe to Spend drafts — designed to spark debate and get shared.

items.intent: Systematically dismantle the three levels of privacy claims — "we encrypt," "we don't sell," and "we never had it" — with real breach data and industry evidence, building to the argument that non-collection is the only defensible position.

items.title: UPI Made Spending Invisible
items.source: IDEAS board: Safe to Spend reframe
items.intent: Industry analysis framing India's UPI success as a half-built revolution: world-class payment rails with zero psychological infrastructure. Uses data (728M daily transactions, 40% impulse buys, 75% personal loan spike) to argue that "Safe to Spend" is the missing emotional layer the ecosystem never built. Analytical tone, systemic argument.

items.source: IDEAS board: Safe to Spend reframe

items.source: IDEAS board — new topic
items.intent: Lead with the punchline — "My privacy policy is 0 words" — then explain why it's literally, architecturally true. Confident and almost playful. Most shareable of the three drafts; the hook works as a standalone image or quote card.
